navSections.title: Memory fundamentals
navSections.blurb: The base layer: addresses, stack vs heap, and how a process sees RAM — foundation for understanding Go’s stack and escape analysis later.

items.label: Memory layout of a program (text, data, heap, stack)

navSections.title: Data representation
navSections.blurb: How values sit in bytes — endianness, alignment, and why `struct` size isn’t always what you expect.

navSections.title: Manual memory management
navSections.blurb: Where C is explicit: allocation, lifetime bugs, and heap fragmentation.

navSections.title: Low-level concurrency
navSections.blurb: Threads and locks without a managed runtime — complements Concurrency (goroutines, channels) with the pthreads / shared-memory picture.

navSections.title: CPU & execution
navSections.blurb: From source to running instructions: compilation, processes, and the call stack.

navSections.title: Files & I/O
navSections.blurb: How user space talks to the kernel for bytes on disk and streams.

navSections.title: Operating system concepts
navSections.blurb: OS-level view of processes, syscalls, and virtual memory — bridges to Cloud Architecture (VMs, containers) and system performance intuition.

navSections.title: Debugging & tooling
navSections.blurb: Practical skills when there’s no safety net — segfaults, cores, and memory checkers.

concepts.title: Pointers
concepts.summary: A pointer stores the memory address of another variable. Use & to take an address, * to read or write through that address.
